title: TOTEM: TOkenized Time series EMbeddings for General Time Series Analysis
authors: Sabera Talukder, Yisong Yue, Georgia Gkioxari (Caltech)
venue: Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR), 12/2024; arXiv:2402.16412 (v2 Jan 2025)
tokenization_method: Self-supervised VQVAE over univariate waveforms. A stack of strided 1D convolutions (dilation=1, non-causal, 2 residual layers) encodes x in R^T into a latent z = E(x) in R^(T/F x D), temporally downsampling by compression factor F=4 with code dimension D=64. Each latent vector is quantized to its nearest codebook entry: z_hat = c_l where l = argmin_i ||z - c_i||_2^2, over a learned codebook C = {c_i}_{i=1}^K of K=256 D-dimensional codewords. A mirror transpose-conv decoder reconstructs x_hat = D(z_hat) in R^T. RevIN (reversible instance normalization) is applied so the VQVAE only has to learn a small set of NORMALIZED waveforms; the per-series mean/std are carried alongside and can be passed to the decoder/forecaster for un-normalization. Trained with the standard VQVAE loss L = ||x - x_hat||_2^2 (reconstruction) + ||sg[z] - z_hat||_2^2 (codebook/VQ) + beta*||z - sg[z_hat]||_2^2 (commitment), beta=0.25, Adam, lr 1e-3. The codebook is FROZEN after VQVAE training and reused unchanged for imputation, anomaly detection, and forecasting. One token = one quantized temporal chunk of length ~F=4 timesteps.
feature_types_handled: numerical / temporal only. Pure univariate real-valued waveforms; multivariate series are handled by FLATTENING every sensor channel into independent univariate series and stacking their token sequences (channel-independent). No categorical inputs, no static covariates, no explicit missing-value embedding (imputation is done by masked reconstruction, not by a missing-token).
token_granularity: temporal patch as a learned discrete code: each token is one of K=256 codebook IDs representing a non-overlapping chunk of T/F latent positions (F=4). NOT per-feature, NOT per-value-bin (unlike Chronos's fixed uniform bins) — the code is a learned D=64 vector chosen by nearest-neighbor in latent space. A length-T_in series becomes T_in/F discrete tokens.
numerical_strategy: RevIN instance-normalize each window, then learn a VQVAE codebook over the normalized waveform; continuous value -> strided-conv latent -> nearest-codeword quantization. This is a LEARNED, distance-aware (Euclidean nearest-neighbor in latent space) discretization, in contrast to fixed uniform binning (Chronos) or quantile bins. Per-series mean/std are predicted/retained separately to un-normalize.
high_cardinality_handling: n/a — no categorical features. The codebook itself is deliberately LOW-cardinality (K=256), chosen over K=512 for parsimony of downstream interactions despite slightly worse reconstruction.
temporal_handling: core of the method: 1D-conv temporal compression by F=4 into a sequence of discrete codes; downstream forecaster is a transformer encoder over the T_in/F tokens with learned temporal positional embeddings, attending along time. Imputation = masked-reconstruction through the same VQVAE; anomaly detection = reconstruction-error scoring through the same VQVAE (no extra downstream model for either). Forecasting predicts a normalized horizon y_bar plus per-series mean mu and std sigma, recombined as y = sigma*y_bar + mu.
scales_to_many_features: Indirect / by stacking only. TOTEM scales to many CHANNELS by treating each as an independent univariate token sequence sharing ONE codebook, so adding channels adds token sequences linearly with no codebook growth — attractive for many homogeneous numeric channels. But it has NO cross-channel attention or feature-interaction mechanism in the tokenizer (cross-series interaction is left entirely to the optional downstream forecaster, which the paper applies per-univariate-series), and NO mechanism for heterogeneous (categorical/static) features. So it does not address the topic's 70+-MIXED-feature problem natively; it addresses many-homogeneous-numeric-channels.
downstream_model: For imputation and anomaly detection: NONE beyond the frozen VQVAE (tasks solved by reconstruction). For forecasting: a transformer-encoder forecaster over the T_in/F=24 tokens (lookback T_in=96) with temporal positional embeddings, plus a separate head predicting per-series mu and sigma; trained supervised with three smooth-L1 losses on {y_bar, mu, sigma}. An MLP forecaster variant was also tested and still beat patch baselines.
pretraining_objective: Self-supervised reconstruction: VQVAE reconstruction + codebook + commitment (beta=0.25) loss, no labels. The codebook is learned once (specialist on one domain OR generalist across many domains jointly) then frozen. Downstream forecaster is trained supervised on top.
key_results: Metric is AvgWins (fraction of dataset/horizon cells where a method is best). Nearly 500 experiments, 3 seeds. FORECASTING (T_in=96, T_out in {96,192,336,720}): specialist in-domain TOTEM 28.6% AvgWins, beating iTransformer 26.8% (TOTEM first in 5 datasets vs iTrans concentrated in electricity+traffic); generalist in-domain 67.9% vs GPT2 33.9%; generalist zero-shot 90.0% vs GPT2 12.5%. IMPUTATION (masks 12.5/25/37.5/50%): specialist 52.1% (GPT2 35.4%, TimesNet 18.8%); generalist in-domain 58.3% vs GPT2 43.8%; zero-shot 80.0% vs 20.0%. ANOMALY DETECTION (adjusted-F1): specialist 33.3% (5-way tie at 13.3%); generalist in-domain 80.0% vs 20.0%; zero-shot 73.3% vs 26.7%. TOKENS-vs-PATCHES ablation (same architecture, TOTEM vs PatchTOTEM): tokens win 67.9% vs 39.3% (specialist), 78.6% vs 23.2% (generalist in-domain), 67.5% vs 35.0% (zero-shot); tokens beat patches for BOTH transformer (67.9 vs 39.3) and MLP (66.1 vs 37.5) forecasters, isolating the gain to the discrete representation, not the forecaster. Permutation test: TOTEM beats GPT2 and PatchTOTEM at p<=0.05.
baselines_compared: Forecasting (14): iTransformer, PatchTST, DLinear, RLinear, TiDE, Crossformer, FEDformer, Non-stationary Transformer, SciNet, GPT2 (gen+spec), N-BEATS, MOMENT, etc. Imputation (17): TimesNet, PatchTST, ETSFormer, FEDformer, Autoformer, Informer, Reformer, LightTS, DLinear, GPT4TS/GPT2, BRITS, CSDI, V-RIN, RDIS. Anomaly detection (19): Anomaly Transformer, TimesNet, GPT4TS, plus flawed and Wu&Keogh-unflawed baselines.
transferable_idea: Learn ONE small frozen VQVAE codebook (K=256, D=64) over RevIN-normalized windows so that a continuous signal becomes a short sequence of distance-aware discrete codes shared across domains, then let any downstream head (transformer OR MLP) operate on code IDs. For 70+-feature tokenization the reusable kernel is: (a) RevIN per-window normalization to decouple scale/level from shape so the codebook only has to model NORMALIZED morphology, carrying mu/sigma as side channels; (b) a learned VQ codebook as a domain-agnostic, distance-aware discretizer that beats fixed binning (Chronos) and raw patches (PatchTST) on these tasks; (c) channel-independence with a SHARED codebook so adding numeric features is linear and the same vocabulary covers all of them. This is a candidate tokenizer for the time-varying numeric half of the feature set.
limitations: (1) Univariate/channel-independent only: no cross-channel or feature-interaction modeling in the tokenizer; multivariate is just stacked univariate sequences. (2) No categorical, no static covariates, no native missing-token — exactly the heterogeneous-fusion problem the topic centers on is unaddressed. (3) F=4 is a fixed, small compression; tokens correspond to ~4-timestep chunks, so the 'token vocabulary' is morphological micro-patterns, not semantically meaningful events. (4) Codebook frozen after pretraining — downstream tasks cannot adapt the vocabulary. (5) Forecaster operates per univariate series, so the headline forecasting wins do not demonstrate joint many-feature modeling. (6) Evaluated only on standard long-horizon TS benchmarks (ETT/weather/electricity/traffic + a few neuro/hydrology zero-shot sets); generalization to wide tabular feature sets is untested.
pitfalls: AvgWins is a count-of-best-cells metric, NOT average error — a method can have the most first-places yet not dominate every cell; e.g. specialist forecasting 28.6% vs iTrans 26.8% is a narrow lead. The strong 'zero-shot 90%' forecasting and 80% imputation numbers are vs a single GPT2 generalist the authors trained, not vs the full specialist baseline panel. Authors deliberately EXCLUDE classification and short-term forecasting and call standard anomaly/classification benchmarks 'leaky' (test set used as validation) and 'flawed' — so the comparison set is curated; treat cross-paper SOTA claims with that caveat. K=256 is chosen for downstream parsimony, NOT reconstruction optimality (K=512 reconstructs better: codebook ablation AvgWins 100% at K=512 vs 0% at K=256). 'Tokenized embeddings' = VQVAE codes over conv latents; no NLP/BPE semantics despite the BPE analogy in the text.
relevance_to_topic: Best-in-route example of a LEARNED vector-quantized tokenizer (vs Chronos's fixed bins) for the time-varying numeric half of a 70+-feature set: a shared frozen codebook + RevIN gives a compact, transferable per-channel vocabulary, but it offers no path to fuse categorical/static features or model cross-feature interaction, which is the topic's hard part.
code_available: yes - https://github.com/SaberaTalukder/TOTEM (371 stars; pushed 2025-02; specialist + generalist scripts for forecasting/imputation/anomaly on ETT/weather/electricity/traffic + MSL/PSM/SMAP/SMD/SWAT; data via Google Drive; no SPDX license declared in repo metadata - check before reuse).
